meta.model: claude-fable-5 (manual, no API key — see CLAUDE.md v4 workaround)

digests.narrative:
His highest-conviction theme survived its first real stress test this cycle. Two SemiAnalysis pieces framing CPO as delayed knocked optical names down 40-60% — and a UDN report (relayed via Serenity) now alleges the same firm launched an institutional photonics ETF into the crash it caused, including names like HIMX it had called overvalued. He read the selloff as manufactured FUD, heavily added to his optical positions at the lows, and reiterated that SIVE and AAOI (his two largest concentrations) are the names he is 'especially confident' in despite the drawdowns.

The core CW-laser chokepoint thesis got its strongest external validation yet: a Nomura report names AXTI and IQE the leading InP players and documents substrate price hikes of 42-78% (2in InP +42-76%, 3in +78%, EML epiwafers +50-75%) — hyperscaler bottlenecking 'confirmed on all fronts from pricing, demand, to supply shortages.' AXTI also signed a 3-year wafer deal with Coherent carrying a $22.3M prepayment for committed capacity. He consolidated the Sivers case into a 'next LITE' summary: O-Net ELS mass production, JBL 1.6T LRO with 'relatively dramatic moats,' the GFS SCALE reference laser (where AMD went for CPO), Ayar/NVLink CPO with Lumentum/Macom reportedly dropped from Ayar's site, plus POET and AEVA starting HVM in H2 2026. The counterweights he acknowledges: SIVE's Bootstrap Europe convertible notes executed (venture debt, 'not a positive thing like others are framing'), and photonics remains hostage to institutional bear notes until earnings validate the ramp.
keyPoints: SemiAnalysis CPO-delay pieces crashed optical names 40-60%; a UDN report alleges the firm then launched a photonics ETF into the drawdown — he added heavily and calls it manufactured FUD.
Nomura validates the InP chokepoint: AXTI/IQE named leading players, substrate price hikes of 42-78% across InP wafers and epiwafers.
AXTI signed a 3-year Coherent wafer deal with a $22.3M prepayment for committed capacity — prepaid supply is what a real bottleneck looks like.
The 'SIVE is the next LITE' summary: O-Net ELS, JBL 1.6T LRO moats, GFS SCALE reference laser, Ayar/NVLink CPO (Lumentum/Macom dropped), POET/AEVA HVM in H2 2026.
SIVE's Bootstrap Europe convertible notes executed — venture debt he concedes is not the positive others frame it as; it remains one of his largest holdings.
He expects thematic photonics/memory/energy/physical-AI outperformance long-run and is explicitly willing to sit through institutional-note drawdowns.

digests.narrative:
The memory upcycle thesis moved from narrative to a pricing schedule this week. UBS expects Q3 2026 DDR contract prices up 32% and Q4 up 18% (versus 17%/12% prior estimates) with NAND flash raised ~30% from the previous quarter; Samsung reportedly plans a 20% DRAM hike in Q3, beating TrendForce's 13-18% forecast. His point: people anchored on the 70-80% hikes of earlier quarters miss that hikes are still compounding on a much higher base. Morgan Stanley sees NAND short into 2027, keeping SNDK/Kioxia-type players and SIMO happy, and remains especially positive on Macronix/Winbond.

On the capex side, MU leads a new ¥1.5T (~$9.3B) investment in Hiroshima — a localized-capex readthrough he flags as bullish for Disco, Advantest, Resonac and Towa. The AXTI InP-substrate thread that anchors this category got its strongest validation yet: a Nomura report names AXTI (with IQE) the leading InP player amid 42-78% substrate price hikes, and a 3-year Coherent wafer deal locked in committed capacity with a $22.3M prepayment. He expects memory-style compounding price hikes to repeat in optical substrates over the next two years of the demand curve.
keyPoints: UBS: Q3 2026 DDR contract prices +32%, Q4 +18% (beating prior 17%/12% estimates); NAND raised ~30% from the previous quarter.
Samsung reportedly plans a 20% DRAM hike in Q3 — above TrendForce's 13-18% forecast; hikes are compounding on an already-elevated base.
Morgan Stanley sees NAND in short supply into 2027 — favorable for SNDK, Kioxia-type players and SIMO upstream.
MU leads a ¥1.5T (~$9.3B) Hiroshima investment — localized capex bullish for Disco, Advantest, Resonac, Towa.
AXTI: Nomura names it a leading InP player amid 42-78% substrate price hikes; 3-year Coherent deal with $22.3M prepayment for committed capacity.
His frame: optical substrates should repeat memory's compounding-price-hike pattern over the next two years.

digests.narrative:
The specialty-foundry leg of the thesis got sell-side backup this week: Kepler upgraded XFAB to Buy, citing lead-customer Melexis demand strengthening 'week-after-week, day-after-day,' AI giving XFAB a structural growth leg (SiC, GaN, photonics) that offsets auto cyclicality, and scarce Western specialty foundries like Silex commanding premium multiples — while XFAB trades at 4.8x 2027 EV/EBITDA versus a 14.5x peer median. His own angle stays one step further out: the photonixfab silicon-photonics line with NVDA as a potential key driver, which he argues traditional firms can't price until volume contracts land.

GlobalFoundries remains the platform beneath his photonics book — the SCALE reference-level laser for hyperscalers spanning pluggable, NPO and CPO, where AMD and others reportedly went for CPO. TSM's silicon-photonics/CPO empire-building (Xintec, VisEra, CoPoS pilot lines) carries forward, now joined by speculation that Samsung Electro-Mechanics and Intel sit in the Boston Dynamics supplier map — the fab layer's first direct link into his physical-AI theme. Equipment names (ASML, AMAT, Lam, KLA, Veeco) remain the unavoidable layer beneath all of it.
keyPoints: Kepler upgrades XFAB to Buy: Melexis demand strengthening weekly, AI as a structural growth leg, cheapest specialty foundry at 4.8x 2027 EV/EBITDA vs 14.5x peer median.
His XFAB angle remains photonixfab silicon photonics with NVDA as potential key driver — unpriceable by traditional models until volume contracts land.
Scarce Western specialty foundries (Silex cited) command higher multiples — a structural re-rating argument for the cohort.
GFS SCALE reference-level laser spans pluggable/NPO/CPO for hyperscalers; AMD and others reportedly went to GFS for CPO.
TSM continues building a silicon-photonics/CPO empire via Xintec and VisEra with CoPoS pilot lines running.
Samsung Electro-Mechanics and Intel appear in the speculated Boston Dynamics supplier map — fabs' first direct link into the physical-AI theme.

digests.narrative:
The 'Meta is cutting capex' narrative he has fought for weeks broke down publicly: Meta's upcoming 'Watermelon' model has reportedly caught up to OpenAI's GPT 5.5 using an order of magnitude more compute than its predecessor, and SemiAnalysis — the same firm whose bear notes hit photonics — says Meta's datacenter and compute will accelerate with 2027 capex 'shockingly high.' His long-standing read (a compute-constraint story, never a demand-weakening one) is aging well, and NBIS remains his highest-conviction pure-play expression of it.

The Anthropic demand wave became this week's concrete catalyst: TeraWulf signed a $19B datacenter lease with Anthropic ('a very positive tailwind for the Neocloud/Colo sector'), and leaked Anthropic docs point to ~1.4GW of Australian capacity worth ~$21.6B — mapping directly onto the sovereign datacenters IREN, SHAZ and other neoclouds have been building there (he discloses no positions in those two). The more speculative fringe — floating/ocean-based datacenters as a future NBIS-style play — he treats as directional color, not thesis.
keyPoints: Meta capex-cut narrative debunked: 'Watermelon' reportedly matches GPT 5.5 on an order of magnitude more compute; SemiAnalysis sees 2027 Meta capex 'shockingly high.'
TeraWulf signs a $19B datacenter lease with Anthropic — a repricing event for the whole neocloud/colo sector's contract value.
Leaked Anthropic docs point to ~1.4GW / ~$21.6B of Australian capacity, mapping onto sovereign DCs built by IREN, SHAZ and peers.
NBIS remains his highest-conviction pure-play neocloud; the demand chain is now corroborated from hyperscalers and frontier labs simultaneously.
Floating/ocean-based datacenters are flagged as an emerging concept (free cooling, geographic constraints) — color, not thesis.

digests.narrative:
Lighter-touch theme, mostly picks-and-shovels research. AEHR's ~100% rally is viewed as largely priced in, with limited near-term re-rating room absent a new catalyst. LPKF (LPK.DE) is a recurring bullish idea on its glass-core substrate monopoly — >80% of major global players selecting its equipment for validation, a 70% market-share target, and NASDAQ-listing discussions — with a stated $3-5B fair value at full volume ramp. Soitec (SOI.PA) is repeatedly cited as a European chokepoint SOI-wafer supplier benefiting from EU CHIPS Act 2.0's new photonics-policy priority, alongside LPKF and XFAB.

ASE Technology (ASX) and Teradyne (TER) round out the packaging/test layer, mentioned inside broader '$10-100B AI-exposed names' baskets as offering compelling ROI versus larger names. Ongoing trade-record research (Starlink and other direct-supplier mapping) continues to surface obscure materials names, and the China->Japan->Korea tungsten (WF6) export-control thread remains a recurring materials-bottleneck watch item.
keyPoints: LPKF (LPK.DE): glass-core substrate monopoly, >80% major-player validation, 70% market-share target, NASDAQ-listing discussions — stated $3-5B fair value at full volume ramp.
Soitec (SOI.PA): European SOI-wafer chokepoint supplier, a repeated beneficiary of EU CHIPS Act 2.0's new photonics-policy priority.
AEHR's ~100% rally is seen as mostly priced in — limited near-term re-rating room without a new catalyst.
ASE Technology (ASX) and Teradyne (TER) round out the packaging/test layer, cited inside broader AI-exposed-name baskets for compelling ROI.
The China->Japan->Korea tungsten (WF6) export-control thread remains a recurring materials-bottleneck watch item.

digests.narrative: The Marvell thread stays sentiment-first: whenever MRVL drops he hears Jensen's 'the next $1T company' comment, and this week he formalized his half-serious 'conspiracy theory' — Nvidia quietly supports the ASIC challengers (MRVL, MediaTek, AlChip, GUC) chipping away at Broadcom's custom-silicon share, the same way neocloud offerings built a moat against CSP custom ASICs last year. He frames it as an interesting ~2-year trade but expects hyperscalers like Google to bring design in-house past 2030, and floats NVDA taking a stake in QCOM next. Nokia recurs as a validating evaluator (with NVDA) of XFAB's silicon-photonics foundry. Still no standalone networking bottleneck thesis — this remains the most narrative-driven category in the corpus.
keyPoints: Formalized 'conspiracy theory': NVDA discretely backs ASIC challengers (MRVL, MediaTek, AlChip, GUC) against Broadcom's custom-silicon share.
Framed as a ~2-year trade opportunity — past 2030 he expects hyperscalers like Google to bring ASIC design in-house.
Jensen's 'next $1T company' comment still anchors MRVL sentiment through drawdowns; he predicts NVDA takes a stake in QCOM next.
Nokia (with NVDA) continues validating XFAB's silicon-photonics foundry for CPO applications.
Still no standalone networking bottleneck thesis — mentions remain list-driven and narrative-first.

digests.narrative: A new, thin entry in this cycle: Corning (GLW) surfaced only inside a broader Bloomberg-driven semis-selloff basket (down ~11.4% alongside AAOI/MRVL/LITE/SNDK/TER on a report UBS called 'not new news'). No dedicated glass-substrate thesis has been posted on it yet — it's tracked here as a natural candidate for the glass-core advanced-packaging wave (alongside SKC Absolics/TSMC CoPoS) given Corning's position as the leading specialty-glass and optical-fiber maker, but that connection hasn't been stated directly by the analyst.
keyPoints: Corning (GLW) has only appeared inside a broader semis-selloff basket so far — no dedicated glass-substrate thesis posted yet.
Tracked as a natural fit for the glass-core advanced-packaging wave given Corning's specialty-glass/fiber leadership, though the analyst hasn't drawn that link explicitly.

digests.narrative:
The physical-AI rotation he was early to is now in full euphoria: LeaderDrive hit all-time highs after a 20% limit-up (his June 5 call at 321 now 495), Unitree's STAR listing was approved, and Agility (CCXI) plus Unitree going public is driving palpable sector excitement. External validation arrived via SVRC Research's 'State of Robotics 2026,' which ranks Agility #2 among US robotics 'national champions' (behind Figure, ahead of Apptronik, Tesla and Boston Dynamics) while warning the US leads where robotics is heading but loses where it ships today — flagging rare-earth exposure and actuator dependency on Japan/Germany/China as the strategic vulnerabilities.

His new structural frame: Amazon is 'the clear winner in the physical AI shift,' and the Amazon ecosystem will catalyze robotics suppliers (Agility foremost) the way Google's TPU program catalyzed suppliers like Lumentum. Chinese VC flows corroborate the theme — ~$13.4B into embodied intelligence/physical AI, with world models 'the biggest consensus in early-stage investment,' where he repeats his AEVA 4D-perception call. A speculated Boston Dynamics supplier map (LG Innotek vision, NVDA Jetson Thor compute, CATL batteries, Korean actuator/reducer names) sketches the picks-and-shovels layer he expects to matter next.
keyPoints: Robotics euphoria: LeaderDrive at ATHs after 20% limit-up, Unitree STAR listing approved, CCXI/Agility and Unitree IPOs driving sector excitement.
SVRC 'State of Robotics 2026' ranks Agility #2 among US national champions — external validation of his top holding's positioning.
The same report flags rare-earth exposure and actuator dependency (Japan/Germany/China) as the US robotics program's critical vulnerabilities.
New frame: Amazon is the clear physical-AI winner — its ecosystem should catalyze robotics suppliers the way Google's TPU program catalyzed LITE.
Chinese VC flows: ~$13.4B into embodied/physical AI; world models are 'the biggest consensus' — AEVA remains his listed 4D-perception expression.
Speculated Boston Dynamics supplier map (LG Innotek, NVDA Jetson Thor, CATL, Korean actuator names) sketches the next picks-and-shovels layer.

digests.narrative:
NVDA's role sharpened from gravitational center to kingmaker this week: his half-serious theory holds that Nvidia discretely supports the ASIC challengers (MRVL, MediaTek, AlChip, GUC) eroding Broadcom's share — 'anything they touch gets brought up' — with a prediction that NVDA takes a stake in QCOM next. AMD's CPO path runs through GlobalFoundries per his SIVE summary post, keeping both accelerator giants tied to the same CW-laser chokepoint that anchors his photonics book.

He also filed a pointed media-narrative observation: when retail buys memory, optical and upstream semi names it's a 'meme stock bubble,' but when institutions buy the identical names it's 'record profits betting on what goes inside AI server racks.' The SemiAnalysis CPO-delay affair (bear notes into a 40-60% optical drawdown, then a photonics ETF launch) reinforced his view that institutional positioning, not fundamentals, drives the short-term tape around the accelerator supply chain.
keyPoints: NVDA as kingmaker: the theory that it quietly backs ASIC challengers (MRVL, MediaTek, AlChip, GUC) against Broadcom, with a QCOM stake predicted next.
AMD reportedly went to GlobalFoundries for CPO — both accelerator giants remain tied to the same CW-laser chokepoint.
Media-narrative asymmetry: retail buying AI supply-chain names is a 'bubble'; institutions buying the same names is 'record profits.'
The SemiAnalysis affair reinforces his read that institutional positioning drives the short-term tape, not fundamentals.
Cerebras (CBRS) starter position and ARM/QCOM datacenter-inference threads carry forward unchanged.

digests.narrative:
The Meta capex war ended decisively in his favor this week: the upcoming 'Watermelon' model has reportedly caught up to OpenAI's GPT 5.5 using an order of magnitude more compute than its predecessor 'Avocado,' Wang clarified Zuckerberg's 'slowdown' comments referred to the industry, not Meta, and SemiAnalysis expects Meta's compute to accelerate with 2027 capex 'shockingly high.' He thanked Bloomberg's Meta piece and the CPO bear notes sarcastically — the misinformation-driven selloff is where he added.

Amazon graduated to his top pick in the physical-AI shift: he expects the Amazon ecosystem to catalyze robotics players the way Google's TPU program catalyzed suppliers like Lumentum, with SVRC listing Amazon Robotics among the US robotics national champions. A new bottleneck thread ties the hyperscalers to the component layer: a high-power cylindrical/BBU battery-cell shortage (Samsung SDI cells via Simplo into META and AMZN datacenters, Panasonic also short) with the clean beneficiaries mostly Korean/Japanese — US readthroughs (VRT, ETN) he flags as adjacent, outside his domain emphasis. GOOGL's compute-constraint fact remains the corpus's cleanest demand-side evidence.
keyPoints: Meta 'Watermelon' reportedly matches GPT 5.5 on 10x the compute of its predecessor — the capex-cut narrative he fought is publicly breaking down.
SemiAnalysis: Meta datacenter/compute will accelerate; 2027 capex 'shockingly high.'
Amazon is his physical-AI winner: its ecosystem should do for robotics suppliers what Google's TPU program did for LITE-style suppliers.
New bottleneck thread: high-power BBU battery-cell shortage feeding META/AMZN datacenters (Samsung SDI, Panasonic short); VRT/ETN only adjacent US readthroughs.
GOOGL cutting Meta's Gemini capacity remains the cleanest demand-side evidence in the corpus.
